title: PRDs from industry and feature specifications
use_when: PRDs from industry and feature specifications. Use when the user needs a product workflow for business analysis related to prds from industry and feature specifications. Trigger terms: pm, prd, requirements, documentation, business-analysis.
do_not_use_when: Do not use PRDs from industry and feature specifications when the request is mainly a different lifecycle artifact; route to the narrower Productize skill that owns that artifact instead. Do not use it when the user lacks enough context to distinguish facts, assumptions, and decision risk.
output_artifact: PRDs from industry and feature specifications delivery brief with scope, requirements, priorities, risks, and acceptance criteria
framework_fit: PRDs from industry and feature specifications fits Delivery work in the Plan lifecycle when the user needs PRDs from industry and feature specifications delivery brief with scope, requirements, priorities, risks, and acceptance criteria and has enough product context to make tradeoffs explicit. Use it to produce a decision-ready artifact, not a framework tour.
failure_modes: Produces PRDs from industry and feature specifications delivery brief with scope, requirements, priorities, risks, and acceptance criteria without tying recommendations to the user's Plan stage, evidence, and decision pressure.
Treats PRDs from industry and feature specifications as generic Delivery advice instead of naming assumptions, missing inputs, risks, and the next validation step.
Fails to route away from PRDs from industry and feature specifications when another Productize skill owns the requested artifact more directly.

examples.prompt: Use $prds-from-industry-and-feature-specifications to turn this prds context into a decision-ready PRDs from industry and feature specifications delivery brief with scope, requirements, priorities, risks, and acceptance criteria.
examples.expected_artifact: PRDs from industry and feature specifications delivery brief with scope, requirements, priorities, risks, and acceptance criteria
